Microbes already signal to us in chemistry

Microbes signal to us through chemistry — and some molecules even mimic our own ligands.

→ GPR43 / GPR109A
Bile acids
→ FXR / TGR5

→ AhR
Some mimic our ligands
commensal GPCR agonists · Cohen 2017

3 / 11

Secreted microbial proteins have no known target

Commensals secrete thousands of small proteins. Almost none have a known human target.

metabolites — mapped
secreted miniproteins — uncharted
If microbes signal to us in chemistry, do they also signal in protein — miniproteins that engage our own receptors directly?
knownunknown

One human hunch, 7,408 structures refolded

From ~2,000 miniprotein families to one named candidate, folded against real receptors and a decoy null.

→
25
human receptors + sequence-matched decoys
→
7,408
structures folded (ESMFold2)
→
1
named candidate: MIMIC
The Claude moment: our first hit scored the precursor, not the secreted form. A human caught the smell of it — Claude Science re-derived every mature peptide, refolded the whole screen in one session, and reranked it.

5 / 11

A gut peptide lands on the IL-7 binding site

Click to build it up — the gut peptide lands on the exact surface where IL-7 binds and SCID mutations cluster. Drag to rotate.

65%of the peptide’s contact epitope overlaps the crystal IL-7 binding site (3DI2)
0.6 ÅIL7R backbone RMSD — the peptide sits in the cytokine groove, 96% within 8 Å of IL-7
4 + 8pathogenic + VUS IL7R residues at that epitope — the Immunodeficiency-104 (SCID) cluster
Not enriched vs receptor background (p = 0.11). The claim is co-location with the functional interface, not enrichment.
⬌ drag the structure to rotate · scroll to zoom
Click to build the interface
IL7R + SCID variant residues (clay)
native IL-7 bound in the cytokine groove
MIMIC-078 — overlaps the same surface
gut peptide 078native IL-7IL7RSCID variant residues

The peptide rides a mobile element into a pathogen

Same Tcp/ArdA conjugative element, same peptide, across 239 gut species — including 15 Clostridioides difficile strains.

p = 0.003
MGE enrichment
(#2 of 592 families)
239 species
Bacillota + Actinomycetota
= horizontal transfer
C. difficile
colitis pathogen
15 strains carry it

7 / 11

We name it MIMIC

It engages IL-7’s receptor with no sequence homology to IL-7 — a structural mimic, carried on a mobile element.

M
Mobile
conjugative element
I
Immuno­modulatory
engages IL7R
M
Modulatory
tunes IL-7 axis
I
ICE-borne
integrative element
C
Commensal
gut microbiome
A structural mimic — not a stolen human gene. MIMIC docks the IL-7 binding site on IL7R (65% overlap), at the surface where human SCID mutations cluster.
mobileimmunecommensal
8 / 11

Immune signaling becomes a transferable trait

The hypothesis: an immune-shielding peptide that blunts IL-7 signaling to keep its microbe invisible — carried on an element that trades that shield between species.

A new class
Not a metabolite, not pattern recognition — a peptide docking a cytokine receptor.
A shield you can trade
Immune invisibility a microbe can acquire by transfer, not only evolve.
A pathogen conduit
A shield selected in commensals, handed to pathogens to help them persist.
9 / 11

One assay settles antagonist versus agonist

The hypothesis: MIMIC blocks IL-7 to keep the microbe invisible. pSTAT5 in IL-7-responsive T cells is the readout.

Arm A — is it an agonist?
MIMIC alone, no IL-7.
Agonist → drives pSTAT5.
Our hypothesis → does nothing here.
Arm B — does it block IL-7?
Fixed IL-7 + MIMIC dose–response.
GO → MIMIC suppresses IL-7 pSTAT5 = immune-shielding antagonist.
+ signal native IL-7 → full pSTAT5
+ blockade anti-IL7R mAb → suppresses
– sequence scrambled MIMIC → no effect
specificity IL-2/IL-15 line → untouched
Go/no-go in a week with off-the-shelf reagents · hypothesis, not yet demonstrated function
10 / 11

The microbiome as a mobile ligand library

MIMIC is one peptide. The screen that found it can read the rest.

If commensals shield themselves by speaking to our immune receptors — and trade that shield on mobile elements — then the gut holds an entire library of human-receptor ligands.
Mobile. Disease-linked. Almost entirely unread. There is an axis of microbe–host communication here we have barely begun to explore.
Open-source · reproducible · Kenneth Loi, Zhaojun Wang · Built with Claude
